title: Chad - Food Security Outlook Update : Refugee impacts are driving income and consumption toward Crisis! (IPC Phase 3!), April - September 2026 (pdf)
summary: During the peak of the lean season (June-August) in  North Kanem and North Barh El-Gazel (BEG), food access for  poor households is expected to deteriorate, resulting in  Crisis (IPC Phase 3). Refugees depend on markets, but are facing food consumption  deficits due to limited income-generating activities and atypically  high prices for imported food commodities. Households will face the combined effects  of increased market dependence, sustained food price increases,  and seasonal declines in income.
bullets: During the peak of the lean season (June-August) in North Kanem and North Barh El-Gazel (BEG), food access for poor households is expected to deteriorate, resulting in Crisis (IPC Phase 3).
Refugees depend on markets, but are facing food consumption deficits due to limited income-generating activities and atypically high prices for imported food commodities.
Households will face the combined effects of increased market dependence, sustained food price increases, and seasonal declines in income.
In most provinces of the central and Sudanian zones, poor households are expected to remain Stressed (IPC Phase 2) through September 2026.

full_text:
Refugee impacts are driving income and consumption toward Crisis! (IPC Phase 3!) 
Key Messages 
This report provides an update to the February 2026 to September 
2026 Food Security Outlook and March 2026 Key Message Update. 
The analysis is based on information available as of April 26, 2026. 
Current anomalies in food security conditions as of April 2026 
Chad - Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026 
Crisis! (IPC Phase 3!) outcomes are expected in the eastern 
departments with high concentrations of refugees (Ouaddaï, 
Sila, Wadi Fira, Ennedi-Est) through September 2026. 
Refugees depend on markets, but are facing food consumption 
deficits due to limited income-generating activities and atypically 
high prices for imported food commodities. Access to food 
assistance is mitigating these deficits, but a small proportion of 
households, fewer than 15 percent, risk deteriorating into 
Emergency (IPC Phase 4). 
Ongoing Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes in Lac Province (Fouli 
and Kaya) are expected to persist through September. 
Persistent insecurity and continued displacement in Lac are 
limiting access to livelihoods for displaced and poor host 
households. During the peak of the lean season (June-August) in 
North Kanem and North Barh El-Gazel (BEG), food access for 
poor households is expected to deteriorate, resulting in 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3). Households will face the combined effects 
of increased market dependence, sustained food price increases, 
and seasonal declines in income. 
In most provinces of the central and Sudanian zones, poor 
households are expected to remain Stressed (IPC Phase 2) 
through September 2026. Food stocks are being depleted 
rapidly, and incomes are insufficient to cover essential non-food 
needs. However, households will maintain minimally adequate 
food consumption through in-kind payments from agricultural 
labor. 
The government estimates that the conflict in the Middle East 
poses limited risks to food supply in Chad. However, the oil 
price shock, in a context where fuel prices are already high 
(2,000 FCFA per liter of gasoline in some eastern localities), could 
affect transport costs, imports, and household purchasing 
power. It is expected to worsen food access difficulties for poor 
households whose incomes will decline during the lean season. 
The massive influx of Sudanese refugees remains the main driver of food insecurity in Chad. Refugees 
are arriving without any means of livelihood. According to the UN High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR), 
FEWS NET is a United States Government-funded activity. The content of this report does not
necessarily reflect the view of the United States Government.
FEWS NET Chad
chad@fews.net
fews.net/west-africa/chad
as of April 27, 2026, around 926,421 new refugees had arrived in the eastern provinces since April 2023, 
particularly in Ennedi-Est, Ouaddaï, Sila, and Wadi Fira. The refugee presence is creating strong competition for 
employment, thereby worsening access to income and food for poor host households. As a result, food demand 
and market prices are also increasing, heightening dependence on food assistance, especially among newly 
arrived refugees. 
The persistence of various conflicts is of concern for worsening food insecurity in Chad. According to 
ACLED, 20 conflict events occurred in Chad between March 13 and April 10, 2026. These incidents caused 26 
deaths and destroyed the livelihoods of 117,188 people. Recurring violence additionally affected 1,191 people, 
forcing displacement and causing movement restrictions in Lac Province. These conditions limit access to 
agricultural activities, fishing, and trade, while placing growing pressure on the livelihoods and resources of host 
communities. A drone attack on March 18 and an armed clash on March 22 between Chadian military forces 
and Sudan’s Rapid Support Forces took place in Ouaddaï Province. This development further restricts cross-
border flows and worsens already fragile food conditions in the eastern provinces. 
Cereal production increased by 12 percent compared to 2024 and by 11.2 percent compared to the five-
year average. According to data revised in March 2026 by the Directorate of Agricultural Production and 
Statistics, cereal production for the 2025/2026 season is estimated at 3,035,565 metric tons, compared with 
2,709,516 metric tons in 2024/25. However, millet and sorghum production, the main staple cereals, 
declined by 3 percent and 17 percent, respectively, compared to the average. The cereal balance sheet 
highlights gross deficits in wheat (99 percent of needs) and local cereals (10 percent of needs), which will have to 
be covered through imports. However, given the closure of the border with Sudan, persistent insecurity in Lac 
Province, and insecurity along the border with the Central African Republic, the local cereal deficit will be 
difficult to offset through imports. 
Local production and commercial stocks are ensuring a satisfactory cereal supply, but food prices are rising 
seasonally compared to the five-year average, except for millet, whose price is 16 percent below average. 
However, the supply of imported food products has declined while their prices have risen sharply. 
Insecurity, the closure of certain borders, and higher transport costs linked to elevated fuel prices have resulted 
in increases of 61 percent for imported rice and 74 percent for wheat flour compared to the average. In the 
eastern provinces, where refugee inflows and the closure of the Sudanese border are generating increased 
demand due to rapid stock depletion, price increases have been particularly severe, exceeding 100 percent. 
Livestock markets are also experiencing strong demand driven by Cameroonian and Nigerian traders and the 
resumption of exports to Libya. Prices remain above the five-year average, and livestock-to-cereal terms of 
trade continue to favor pastoralists. 
Poor household incomes, mainly derived from the sale of local cereals, are atypically below average due 
to reduced millet and sorghum production and exceptionally low prices for millet and bérébéré (dry-
season sorghum). To cope with school expenses and debt repayment, households increased millet sales, 
despite millet being their main source of food and income. These large-scale sales created an oversupply in the 
market during the harvest period, driving prices down. The exceptionally abundant bérébéré harvest also failed 
to help households rebuild stocks because they were forced to sell large quantities for very little income. In Am 
Timan, in Salamat Province, an important bérébéré production zone, local markets became saturated, causing 
prices to collapse. The price of a 100 kilogram bag of berbere, which sold for as much as 35,000 FCFA in 2025, 
dropped to between 4,000 and 6,000 FCFA in March 2026. 
The conflict in the Middle East presents limited risks of food shortages in Chad, according to the 
government and the National Council of Chadian Importers, Investors, Industrialists, and Exporters (CONITE), 
because the country has diversified sources of supply. In the immediate term, the impact is reflected in reduced 
remittances from Chadians working in the Gulf. However, the country continues to face already high fuel prices 
(2,000 FCFA, or about 3.40 USD, per liter of gasoline in some eastern localities), which chronically affect 
transport costs, imports, and household purchasing power. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 2
Humanitarian food assistance 
Updates to key assumptions about atypical food security conditions through September 2026 
Most of the assumptions that underpinned FEWS NET’s analysis of 
the most likely scenario for the Chad February 2026 to September 
2026 Food Security Outlook remain valid; however, 
the following updates have been made to incorporate new 
evidence: 
Humanitarian food assistance 
The food security component of the assistance plan was funded at only 4.4 percent in April. It is part of a 
broader humanitarian assistance plan developed in February by the government and its partners. The plan aims 
to provide humanitarian assistance to 3.4 million people at a total cost of 984.2 million USD, but it was only 22.5 
percent funded as of April 17, 2026. On April 11, 2026, a joint report by UNHCR and WFP warned about 
insufficient funding for refugees. The negative effects are already visible in refugee camps in eastern Chad, 
where food rations were cut by half during the first quarter. 
Figure 1 
Rainfall anomaly, March 20-April 20, 
2026 
Source: USGS 
The influx of Sudanese refugees and Chadian returnees 
into the eastern provinces is expected to continue 
throughout the projection period. Despite a recent relative 
decline in monthly refugee inflows, the security situation in 
Sudan’s Darfur and Kordofan regions remains volatile. Clashes 
between the Rapid Support Forces and the Sudanese army are 
expected to continue near El-Geneina and Adré (Darfur), with 
sporadic spillovers into Chadian territory. 
Although average to above-average rainfall is still 
expected across all agropastoral zones of Chad, there is a 
high risk of flooding, particularly in the Sudanian zone, 
between July and September 2026. 
With the rains already beginning in the Sudanian zone and the 
southern Sahel (Figure 1), transhumance toward pastoral 
areas is expected to accelerate to avoid an atypical 
resurgence of farmer-herder conflicts. Localized increases in 
conflict are possible along transhumance corridors 
between April and May. A relative calm is expected from June 
onward, with the completion of transhumance movements toward northern pastoral areas. 
The national pasture balance sheet, published in March 2026, remains in surplus through the end of April 
(259 percent of needs), before declining between May and June (109 percent of needs), though still showing 
significant localized deficits in Lac, Kanem, BEG, Batha, Wadi Fira, and Ennedi-Est. 
The global oil shock could increase the cost of agricultural inputs, transport, and imports, heightening 
risks to food security. Accelerated price inflation is anticipated, driven by above-average seasonal increases in 
imported goods, particularly in urban centers and high-demand areas of the eastern provinces. In addition, 
rising energy prices in Gulf countries, which play a key role in nitrogen fertilizer production, are expected to 
trigger sharp increases in fertilizer prices and reduce fertilizer availability. 
The national response plan for households facing food insecurity during the 2026 lean season includes cash 
transfers totaling 53.6 billion FCFA for 1,005,773 people. It also provides humanitarian food assistance in kind 
valued at 67.9 billion FCFA for 1,376,189 people. However, the population targeted by this assistance consists 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 3
Projected acute food insecurity outcomes through September 2026 
Crisis! (IPC Phase 3!) outcomes are expected through September 2026 in departments with high 
concentrations of refugees: Assoungha (Ouaddaï), Kimiti (Sila), Wadi Hawar (Ennedi-Est), Dar-Tama, and Kobé 
(Wadi Fira). These provinces continue to experience an influx of refugees and returnees from Sudan, along with 
atypically high prices for imported food products. Host households are experiencing deteriorating food access due to 
the combined effects of early depletion of food stocks, strong market dependence, persistently high food prices, and 
declining income-earning opportunities. The massive influx of refugees is placing increasing pressure on local 
resources and further weakening the coping capacities of poor host households. Refugees, who are arriving without 
livelihoods, are facing severe food consumption deficits and depend heavily on food assistance, which is preventing 
Emergency (IPC Phase 4) outcomes. Due to insufficient income, a growing proportion of households will increasingly 
resort to begging to survive. A share of these households, estimated at less than 15 percent of the population, is 
vulnerable to deteriorating into Emergency (IPC Phase 4). 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes are also expected through September 2026 in the remaining departments of 
Ouaddaï, Sila, Wadi Fira, and Ennedi-Est provinces, where the refugee presence is more limited. Poor households 
will depend exclusively on markets in a context of high prices. Seasonal declines in income and reduced borrowing 
opportunities will further constrain food access. Poor households will reduce the number of meals consumed and 
face food consumption deficits. At the peak of the lean season (between July and August), a small proportion of these 
households (around 5 percent) may resort to extreme coping strategies, particularly begging, and deteriorate into 
Emergency (IPC Phase 4). 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes are anticipated through September in Fouli and Kaya departments, affected by 
conflict in Lac Province. The deterioration of livelihoods, slowdown in markets, and near halt of cross-border trade 
will reduce incomes for host households and internally displaced persons (IDPs). This decline limits the amount of 
food accessible on markets and increases reliance on negative coping strategies, including consumption of cheaper 
foods, reducing meals, and borrowing food. 
Crisis (IPC Phase 3) outcomes are also expected between June and September 2026 in North Kanem and North 
BEG. Seasonal increases in food prices, amplified by rising transport costs and deteriorating roads, will coincide with 
declining incomes linked to lower livestock prices and reduced sales of gathered products. Poor households are 
therefore likely to experience food consumption deficits. 
Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes are expected to persist through September 2026 in the northern provinces 
(Borkou, Ennedi-Ouest, and Tibesti). Poor households in these provinces are expected to maintain minimally 
adequate food consumption throughout the projection period due to income from cross-border trade, despite their 
market dependence and anticipated increases in imported food prices. 
In the central and Sudanian zones, poor households in Salamat, Moyen-Chari, Tandjilé, and Guéra are 
expected to experience Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes beginning in April 2026. Residual stocks from bérébéré 
production and limited income from fruit sales will allow households to meet minimum food, but not non-food, 
needs. 
From June through September, Stressed (IPC Phase 2) outcomes are expected to expand across most 
departments in the central and Sudanian zones. Poor households will face widespread depletion of food stocks, 
rising prices, and sharply reduced purchasing power due to weak income from agricultural labor and limited 
borrowing opportunities. Households will be able to maintain limited food consumption due to in-kind payments, but 
will have no remaining income to cover essential non-food needs. 
only of host households in need during the lean season. For Sudanese refugees, the WFP plans to resume 
monthly food assistance distributions through July/August. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 4

Each of these maps adheres to IPC v3.1 humanitarian assistance mapping protocols and flags where significant 
levels of humanitarian assistance are being/are expected to be provided. indicates that at least 25 percent of 
households receive on average 25-50 percent of caloric needs from humanitarian food assistance (HFA). 
indicates that at least 25 percent of households receive on average over 50 percent of caloric needs through 
HFA. This mapping protocol differs from the (!) protocol used in the maps at the top of the report. The use of (!) 
indicates areas that would likely be at least one phase worse in the absence of current or programmed 
humanitarian assistance. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 6
Recommended citation: FEWS NET. Chad Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026: Refugee impacts are driving 
income and consumption toward Crisis! (IPC Phase 3!), 2026. 
Food Security Outlook Update 
This Food Security Outlook Update provides an analysis of current acute food insecurity conditions and any changes to FEWS NET's latest projection of acute food insecurity outcomes 
in the specified geography over the next six months. Learn more here. 
* FEWS NET classification is IPC-compatible with limited exceptions. IPC-compatible analysis follows key IPC protocols but does not necessarily reflect the consensus of national food security 
partners. As of IPC 3.0, the IPC no longer assesses the impact of food assistance on classification and thus no longer maps the (!). However, FEWS NET continues to produce food security 
maps inclusive of the (!) as well as maps compatible with IPC 3.0/3.1, which include the mapping of food security assistance bags. In rare cases, FEWS NET classification is IPC-aligned but 
not IPC-compatible. IPC-aligned analysis is evidence-based but some types of evidence required for IPC-compatibility were not available. 
Food Security Outlook Update April - September 2026
Famine Early Warning Systems Network 7

related.title: Aligning the U.S. and Canadian Defense Industrial Bases
related.summary: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but  by the ability to produce at scale . After decades of  reliance on globalized supply chains  for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the  Defense Production Act  to  incentivizing private capital  investments and  even selective government equity stakes . For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
bullets: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale .

After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes .

For many countries that have pursued efficiency-driven globalization for decades, the economic pull of China’s market makes unilateral disengagement difficult, even when national security concerns are clear.
Luckily, for many countries, allied markets represent a significantly larger combined economic opportunity than China alone.

The challenge, therefore, is not simply one of dependence, but of working together through aligned demand, investment, and industrial policy to create markets and demand signals that can compete with the gravitational pull of China’s economy.
related.excerpt: The United States and Canada are both racing to rebuild their defense industrial bases, recognizing that future conflicts will be determined not only by military capability, but by the ability to produce at scale . But they cannot succeed alone — and importantly, they do not need to start from scratch. After decades of reliance on globalized supply chains for everything from consumer products to critical defense technologies, the United States is reasserting a more active industrial policy, using tools ranging from the Defense Production Act to incentivizing private capital investments and even selective government equity stakes . Canada is undergoing a parallel shift, with increased defense spending commitments, the recent release of its first Defence Industrial Strategy , and the newly launched Defence Investment Agency . These efforts create significant opportunity, but also carry risk. While the United States and Canada already share deep and often underappreciated industrial integration, in some sectors, this collaboration is applied late or unevenly. Without more deliberate alignment early in the process, the two countries may duplicate investments, compete for scarce labor a

related.title: Western Withdrawal, Jihadist Expansion: How the Sahel Became Ground Zero for Global Terrorism
related.summary: The United States completed its  withdrawa l  from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement. On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. French forces  withdrew  from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.
bullets: The United States completed its withdrawa l from Niger in September 2024, abandoning key drone and intelligence hubs after its military government terminated their bilateral agreement.
On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali.
French forces withdrew from Mali in 2022 after the military junta demanded its exit, ending Operation Barkhane.

The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain events in what has become the world’s most lethal theatre of jihadist violence.
From Intervention to Expulsion Over the past three years, Western military presence and influence across the central Sahel have collapsed.
related.excerpt: On April 25, armed groups launched near-simultaneous attacks against military installations and key strategic sites across Mali. Claimed by Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin , a jihadist group, and conducted in coordination with Tuareg separatist forces from the Front de libération de l’Azawad, the attacks targeted multiple nodes across the country’s security architecture simultaneously, from the capital Bamako to Gao, Mopti, and Kidal. While the attacks themselves were a shock, they should be understood as the logical endpoint of a deteriorating security trajectory that Western governments have watched from an ever-increasing distance. The geographic scope of the attacks, the targeting of senior officials, and the coordination between jihadist and separatist forces reflect a step change in insurgent capability that has been building for years. Western governments now face a predicament of their own making. As the regional security environment has worsened, their capacity to respond has diminished in direct proportion. The systematic breakdown of military and diplomatic relationships with Sahelian states has left Western actors increasingly unable to see, shape, or contain event

related.title: Israeli security minister stirs diplomatic outrage with flotilla activist abuse video
related.summary: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government. “We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.”  New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on. Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
bullets: Israeli minister Ben-Gvir posts video taunting detained Gaza flotilla activists – video Italy’s prime minister, Giorgia Meloni, said the images were “unacceptable”, and demanded the release of all Italian citizens involved, along with an apology for the mistreatment and the display of “total contempt” toward the Italian government.

“We condemn the actions of Israeli minister Ben-Gvir – who Australia has sanctioned – and the degrading actions of Israeli authorities towards those detained.” New Zealand’s foreign affairs minister, Winston Peters, instructed the ministry to call in the Israeli ambassador so his “grave concerns” could be directly passed on.

Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid.
“The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement.
The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”.
related.excerpt: Israel’s far-right national security minister, Itamar Ben-Gvir, has sparked a diplomatic crisis by publishing footage of Israeli security forces abusing international activists who were detained as they tried to sail to Gaza with aid. Three activists were taken to hospital as result of Israeli violence, lawyers representing the group said. They were subsequently discharged. Dozens of others have suspected broken ribs, resulting in breathing problems. “The team reports systemic violations of due process, and widespread physical and psychological abuse by Israeli authorities,” the rights group Adalah said in a statement. There were “a large number of complaints of extreme violence”. Ben-Gvir’s video drew a rapid and furious response from countries whose citizens were onboard the boats, including the UK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Spain, Ireland, Australia and New Zealand, in many cases from the top of government. The US ambassador to Israel, Mike Huckabee, one of the country’s staunchest allies, described Ben-Gvir’s behaviour as “despicable” and said the minister had “betrayed the dignity of his nation”. related.excerpt: KYIV, Ukraine — Senior White House officials publicly placed Ukraine’s military ahead of allied counterparts in Europe across four venues last week, and in some respects ranked Kyiv ahead of the United States itself, even as U.S. President Donald Trump has continued to dismiss Ukraine’s military strength. The Ukrainian armed forces are “the strongest, most powerful armed forces in all of Europe,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio said last week, citing a five-to-one Russian-to-Ukrainian casualty rate and four years of battlefield adaptation. The necessity of fighting the war, Rubio said, has pushed Ukrainians to develop “new tactics, new techniques, new equipment, new technology that is creating a sort of hybrid asymmetrical warfare.” U.S. Army Secretary Dan Driscoll told the Senate Armed Services Committee the same week that Ukraine has fused drones, sensors and weapons into a single command network running across the front, while U.S. Army systems remain “compartmentalized, isolated and ineffective against modern threats.” “Ukraine’s Delta common operating system, their modular open system architecture command and control system, is absolutely incredible,” Driscoll testified. “It fu

related.title: Ukraine and Russia Are Souring on U.S. Negotiations
related.summary: Yet the Trump administration has in some ways pulled its punches when it comes to Kyiv: Although it has decreased the volume of aid it gives to Ukraine, it still sells weapons to the country via NATO, provides Kyiv with intelligence support, and sanctions Russian oil (though those sanctions have been  partially lifted ). Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later  waxing poetic  about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone. In March, Lavrov  said  the spirit of Anchorage was evaporating; and in April, he said  negotiations  were not Moscow’s “top priority.” “It’s pretty clear they’re down on [negotiations],” said John Herbst, senior director of the Atlantic Council’s Eurasia Center.
bullets: Yet the Trump administration has in some ways pulled its punches when it comes to Kyiv: Although it has decreased the volume of aid it gives to Ukraine, it still sells weapons to the country via NATO, provides Kyiv with intelligence support, and sanctions Russian oil (though those sanctions have been partially lifted ).

Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later waxing poetic about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone.

In March, Lavrov said the spirit of Anchorage was evaporating; and in April, he said negotiations were not Moscow’s “top priority.” “It’s pretty clear they’re down on [negotiations],” said John Herbst, senior director of the Atlantic Council’s Eurasia Center.
pressure —but that the United States believed Russia would eventually take the Donbas in time, which would then reopen the way for a negotiated peace deal.

related.excerpt: Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later waxing poetic about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone. On Wednesday, top Russian foreign-policy official Yuri Ushakov told Russian media: “I don’t know about the ‘spirit of Anchorage.’ I’ve never used that phrase.” Last August, Russia triumphantly presented a summit with the United States in Alaska as a breakthrough in its war to conquer Ukraine, later waxing poetic about the “spirit of Anchorage.” Almost a year later, that spirit is gone. related.excerpt: Senior military special operations forces leaders from the U.S., Sweden and the Philippines participated in a morning panel today about the importance of SOF partnerships and alliances, as part of Special Operations Forces Week 2026 in Tampa, Florida. During the one-hour conversation, the four participants discussed how building trust through international alliances enhances collective readiness and interoperability, making it a prerequisite for mission success in contested environments. "I think the most important thing is to continue to build trust between partners within the alliance, [and] also between partners," Swedish army Brig. Gen. Rickard Johansson, commander of Sweden's special operations forces, replied after the panel was asked what the international SOF community needs to focus on most to ensure that alliances are stronger, truly integrated and capable of meeting the challenges of an increasingly complex and contested world. "And we will continue to do that, because that is the bottom line for all activities that we are supposed to do: build trust," Johansson added. Air Force Maj. Gen. Claude K. Tudor Jr., commander of U.S. Special Operations Command Africa, concurred

related.title: Calls to Uphold International Law Dominate Security Council Debate on Civilian Protection, as Delegates Demand Safety for Journalists, Aid Workers
related.summary: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. Decade after Resolution 2286 (2016), Medical, Humanitarian Workers under Increasing Attack In 2025 alone, she noted, the UN recorded more than 1,350 attacks on medical care across 18 conflicts. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in  Ukraine  and infrastructure in the  United Arab Emirates .
bullets: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result.
Decade after Resolution 2286 (2016), Medical, Humanitarian Workers under Increasing Attack In 2025 alone, she noted, the UN recorded more than 1,350 attacks on medical care across 18 conflicts.
Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in Ukraine and infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates .

Rather, it results from the choice made by conflicting parties to ignore their obligations to protect civilians, to adopt increasingly permissive interpretations of international law, to subordinate the protection of civilians to claims of military necessity or to allow impunity prevail.
The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”.
related.excerpt: While civilians continue to suffer because parties to conflict choose to ignore their legal obligation to protect them, the Security Council heard today that adhering to the rules of war — and enforcing accountability when they are broken — can produce a different result. The Council is holding its annual day-long open debate on the protection of civilians in armed conflict as the United Nations observes the ninth iteration of its “ Protection of Civilians Week ”. Just yesterday, 19 May, the organ met following attacks on civilian populations in Ukraine and infrastructure in the United Arab Emirates . “One civilian was killed approximately every 14 minutes in 2025,” said Edem Wosornu, Director of the Crisis Response Division in the Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs (OCHA). All too often, civilians are not collateral damage — “they are the target”, she stressed. Explosive weapons tear through towns and cities, destroying lives and the infrastructure that sustains them. And healthcare is also under attack, as she observed that — 10 years after the adoption of resolution 2286 (2016) on protecting medical care in conflict — “the situation has only gotten worse”. related.title: Italy rethinks EU defense-financing aid as arms spending falls out of fashion
related.summary: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
bullets: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target.

In a letter to Von der Leyen on Sunday, Meloni said it was imperative to use state cash to soften the blow of rising energy costs — and the consequent hike in the cost of living — caused by the current slowdown of oil supplies to Italy due to the blockage of the Strait of Hormuz.
Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry.
He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response.
Meloni will nevertheless need to explain to NATO partners why she is considering passing on EU plans to boost defense spending at the next alliance summit in Turkey on July 7-8.
related.excerpt: ROME — Italy is considering backing out of a second EU-supported boost to defense spending, taking to about €27 billion ($31 billion) the amount of rearmament cash it could forego as it focuses on tackling soaring energy costs. Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni has told EU Commission chief Ursula von der Leyen that Italy may pass on the offer of €14.9 billion in cheap, so-called SAFE loans from Brussels which could prove essential to pushing Italian defense spending towards NATO’s five percent of GDP target. Italy’s defense minister Guido Crosetto said the final decision to use or not use SAFE loan needed to be taken by the end of May, and the last word needed to come from Italy’s finance ministry. He said he had written twice to finance minister Giancarlo Giorgetti asking what Giorgetti planned to do, but had yet to receive a response. The suggestion by Meloni is the second time in two months she has publicly mulled passing on an EU-driven way to push up spending. In April she suggested Italy will not take advantage of an EU scheme allowing Rome to exempt a quantity of defense spending from calculations of annual deficit spending in order to allow extra arms purchases without
